content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://newsroom.heart.org/news/8-hour-time-restricted-eating-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular-death", "title": "8-hour time-restricted eating linked to a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death", "summary": "PRIMARY AHA press release (March 18, 2024) announcing abstract P192 by Zhong et al. at the AHA Epidemiology and Prevention | Lifestyle and Cardiometabolic Scientific Sessions 2024 in Chicago. Analysis of approximately 20,000 U.S. adults from NHANES 2003-2018, linked to the CDC National Death Index through December 2019, with median follow-up 8 years (maximum 17). Participants who ate all food within a window of less than 8 hours per day had a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death compared with those eating across 12-16 hours. Among adults with existing cardiovascular disease, an eating window of 8 to less than 10 hours was associated with a 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke. Time-restricted eating did not reduce all-cause mortality. Eating more than 16 hours per day was associated with lower cancer mortality among cancer patients. The release explicitly notes findings are preliminary (not peer-reviewed), acknowledges reliance on two 24-hour self-reported dietary recalls, and that association does not imply causation. Senior author Victor Wenze Zhong (Shanghai Jiao Tong University). Commentary by Christopher D. Gardner (Stanford) urges caution about nutrient quality and baseline differences.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health", "Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["Approximately 20,000 U.S. adults (average age 49) from NHANES 2003-2018 followed through December 2019, median 8 years (max 17 years)", "Eating window <8 hours/day associated with 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death vs 12-16 hour window", "Among adults with existing CVD, 8 to <10 hour eating window associated with 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke", "Time-restricted eating did not reduce all-cause mortality; >16 hours/day linked to lower cancer mortality among cancer patients", "Senior author Victor Wenze Zhong, Shanghai Jiao Tong University; presented at AHA EPI|Lifestyle Scientific Sessions 2024, March 18-21, Chicago"], "knowledge_contribution": "PRIMARY source for the 91% claim. Establishes the exact study design, population, follow-up window, effect sizes, and explicit limitations acknowledged by the AHA (self-reported recall, two-day dietary snapshot, preliminary/not peer-reviewed, observational/not causal). Anchor document for downstream verification."}, {"url": "https://www.heart.org/en/news/2024/03/18/time-restricted-eating-may-raise-cardiovascular-death-risk-in-the-long-term", "title": "Time-restricted eating may raise cardiovascular death risk in the long term", "summary": "AHA News article by Laura Williamson (March 18, 2024) summarizing the same Zhong et al. abstract in plainer language. Reports that people restricting eating to less than 8 hours per day were nearly twice as likely to die of cardiovascular disease (combining heart disease and stroke) within a median 8-year follow-up, compared with those eating across 12-16 hours, in an analysis of 20,078 U.S. adults averaging 49 years old. The elevated mortality was seen in the general population and among those with pre-existing CVD or cancer. All-cause and cancer-specific mortality were not significantly affected, though cancer patients eating more than 16 hours/day had lower cancer mortality. Notably includes independent commentary from Dr. Kenneth Mukamal (Beth Israel Deaconess / Harvard Medical School) who was NOT involved: he cautions this is observational, unmeasured confounders may drive the association (e.g., cancer patients may eat less due to appetite loss), and advises against abandoning time-restricted eating for weight management on the basis of this single abstract. Mukamal recommends proven patterns like DASH, Mediterranean, and pescetarian diets.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health", "Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["20,078 U.S. adults analyzed, average age 49, median follow-up 8 years", "Eating window <8 hours/day associated with nearly double the CVD mortality risk vs 12-16 hour window", "Dr. Kenneth Mukamal (Beth Israel Deaconess / Harvard Medical School) flagged unmeasured confounding and reverse causation as alternative explanations", "No effect on all-cause mortality; >16 hr eating window linked to lower cancer mortality among cancer patients", "Mukamal recommends DASH, Mediterranean, and pescetarian diets as proven heart-healthy patterns per AHA scientific statement"], "knowledge_contribution": "SECONDARY AHA-News retelling of the primary abstract. Adds independent Harvard commentary that contextualizes the 91% figure, explicitly names the observational/confounding limitations, and recommends evidence-based alternatives. Useful for balanced reporting of risk signal."}, {"url": "https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2817556", "title": "Study Examines Intermittent Fasting and Cardiovascular Mortality", "summary": "Short JAMA Medical News item summarizing the Zhong et al. AHA conference abstract. Reports that limiting eating to an 8-hour window or less per day was linked to a 91% increased risk of death from cardiovascular disease compared with eating across 12-16 hours, based on preliminary observational data presented at an AHA conference. The note emphasizes that multiple prior studies had found health benefits when people shortened their eating windows, but the new analysis found no association with lower all-cause mortality. Despite being hosted on JAMA Network, this entry is a brief news blurb (only a few hundred characters of substantive content), not the primary study nor a peer-reviewed JAMA paper, and contains no additional methodology or effect-size detail beyond the headline 91% figure and the comparison groups. Serves mainly as independent confirmation that a reputable medical outlet is reporting the same headline claim.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health", "Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Eating window of 8 hours or less per day linked to 91% increased cardiovascular mortality risk vs 12-16 hour window", "Data presented at an American Heart Association conference as preliminary observational research", "Time-restricted eating was not associated with lower all-cause mortality in this study", "Notes that multiple prior studies had reported health benefits of shortened eating windows"], "knowledge_contribution": "SECONDARY short-form news coverage from JAMA Medical News confirming that the 91% CVD-mortality figure is being reported by mainstream medical journalism. Adds no new methodology but documents the tension with prior benefit-finding literature."}, {"url": "https://epocrates.com/online/article/aha-2024-intermittent-fasting-linked-to-a-91-higher-risk-of-cardiovascular", "title": "AHA 2024: Intermittent fasting linked to a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death", "summary": "Epocrates clinical news summary of the same Zhong et al. abstract presented at AHA EPI|Lifestyle Scientific Sessions 2024 (March 18-21, Chicago). Restates that over 20,000 U.S. adults who ate within less than 8 hours per day had a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death than those eating across 12-16 hours. Adds that among adults with existing cardiovascular disease, an 8 to less than 10 hour eating window was associated with 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke. Time-restricted eating did not reduce all-cause mortality; eating more than 16 hours/day was associated with lower cancer mortality among cancer patients. Replicates study design details (NHANES 2003-2018 linked to CDC National Death Index through December 2019, two 24-hour dietary recalls, median 8-year follow-up, max 17 years, average age 49, demographic breakdown 73.3% non-Hispanic White, 11% Hispanic, 8% non-Hispanic Black, 6.9% other). Includes Christopher Gardner's (Stanford) commentary acknowledging short-term benefits but questioning nutrient density and reliance on only two days of dietary data. Explicitly cites the AHA press release as source.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health", "Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["Approximately 20,000 U.S. adults, average age 49, NHANES 2003-2018 linked to CDC National Death Index through December 2019", "Eating window <8 hours/day: 91% higher cardiovascular death risk vs 12-16 hour window", "Among existing CVD patients, 8 to <10 hour eating window: 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke", "Demographic: 73.3% non-Hispanic White, 11% Hispanic, 8% non-Hispanic Black, 6.9% other; approximately 50/50 male/female", "Source cited: AHA press release at newsroom.heart.org, Abstract P192 from EPI|Lifestyle Scientific Sessions 2024"], "knowledge_contribution": "SECONDARY clinician-facing summary that faithfully replicates the AHA press release including the study population, demographic stratification, and the 66% subgroup finding. Useful cross-check that the headline 91% and 66% figures are consistently reported across healthcare media channels."}, {"url": "https://www.sciencemediacentre.org/expert-reaction-to-conference-abstract-about-time-restricted-eating-and-cardiovascular-death/", "title": "Expert reaction to conference abstract about time-restricted eating and cardiovascular death", "summary": "EXPERT COMMENTARY roundup from the UK Science Media Centre following the Zhong et al. abstract. Six independent experts weigh in, and the consensus is strongly cautionary: because the full paper has not been peer-reviewed and only a conference abstract is available, robust conclusions are not possible. Dr. Baptiste Leurent (UCL Medical Statistics) says the doubling of CVD risk is striking but may be explained by baseline differences or healthier lifestyles of those following the diet. Prof. Sir David Spiegelhalter (Cambridge) bluntly states 'this abstract should not have been graced with a press release,' questioning why only two days were used to classify long-term eating patterns. Prof. Kevin McConway (Open University) warns there is 'just about enough in the conference abstract to throw huge doubts' on whether the study can show what it claims, highlights reverse causation (people already worried about CV health may choose to restrict eating), and notes confounders are unmeasured. Prof. Keith Frayn (Oxford) notes body weights and medical history of participants are unknown. Prof. Tom Sanders (King's College London) points out likely confounding by shift work (truck drivers, healthcare workers) which independently elevates CVD and T2D risk. Dr. Duane Mellor (Aston) emphasizes socioeconomic confounders (poverty, stress) and warns against 'concerning headlines' from such limited data. The study is titled 'Association Between Time-Restricted Eating and All-Cause and Cause-Specific Mortality' by Meng Chen et al.", "perspectives": ["Methodological", "Public_Health", "Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Conference abstract title: 'Association Between Time-Restricted Eating and All-Cause and Cause-Specific Mortality' by Meng Chen et al., not peer-reviewed", "Prof. Sir David Spiegelhalter (Cambridge): 'This abstract should not have been graced with a press release,' citing only two days of dietary data to classify long-term eating patterns", "Prof. Kevin McConway (Open University): reverse causation possible, since people worried about CV health may have adopted restricted eating; confounders not disclosed", "Prof. Tom Sanders (King's College London): probable confounding by antisocial shift-work hours (truck drivers, night workers, healthcare), which independently raises CVD and type 2 diabetes risk", "Dr. Baptiste Leurent (UCL): results suggest approximately twice the CVD death risk, but limited abstract prevents robust conclusions"], "knowledge_contribution": "EXPERT COMMENTARY essential for downweighting the 91% headline. Provides six independent methodological critiques: (1) abstract not peer-reviewed, (2) two-day dietary recall insufficient to classify long-term eating patterns, (3) unmeasured confounders (shift work, socioeconomic status, baseline health), (4) reverse causation plausible, (5) body weight and medical history not reported. Central document for a balanced risk assessment."}, {"url": "https://www.facebook.com/WKRG.News.5/posts/a-study-found-that-people-who-followed-a-time-restricted-eating-schedule-had-a-9/1183251893841569/", "title": "Facebook", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT: paywalled/shell/social media", "perspectives": ["Public_Health"], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": "Facebook login wall; no extractable post content. Indicates social-media amplification of the AHA 91% headline by local TV news WKRG but adds no verifiable evidence."}]}
